items.name: Anansi and the Pot of Wisdom

sections.The Story:
Anansi had a problem.

He'd gathered ALL the wisdom in the world into a big clay pot. Every clever thought, every good idea, every solution to every problem — all in one pot.

Now he wanted to hide it at the top of a tall tree, where no one else could reach it.

He tied the pot to his FRONT and started climbing. But the pot kept bumping the trunk. He slipped. He scrambled. He fell.

His small son watched from below.

'Father,' the boy said, 'if you tied the pot to your BACK, it wouldn't bump the tree.'

Anansi looked down. He looked at the pot. He looked at the tree.

The boy was right.

Anansi was so startled that he dropped the pot. It smashed on the ground. All the wisdom scattered everywhere — into the rivers, the forests, the villages. That's why wisdom is found everywhere, in everyone, and no one person has all of it.

But here's the hidden teaching: Anansi had a problem and he tried one approach. It didn't work. His son saw the problem differently and offered a solution.

This is problem solving:
1. What's the problem? (The pot bumps the tree.)
2. What have I tried? (Tying it to the front.)
3. What else could I try? (Tying it to the back.)
4. Pick one and test it.
5. Did it work? If not, try the next idea.

Anansi was so attached to his first idea that he couldn't see the simple answer. His son, with fresh eyes, could.
sections.The Song:
What's the problem? What's the plan?
Try one thing and try again.
Fresh eyes see what stuck eyes miss —
Every problem starts like this.
sections.The Skill: This story teaches PROBLEM SOLVING — the step-by-step skill of naming a problem, brainstorming solutions, picking one, testing it, and adjusting. Sometimes the answer is simple. Sometimes you need someone else's fresh eyes. The key is: don't keep banging the pot against the tree.
sections.For Parents: DBT Skill: PROBLEM SOLVING (Emotion Regulation). This skill is used AFTER checking the facts — when the emotion fits the situation and the situation CAN be changed. Steps: (1) Describe the problem specifically, (2) Check the facts, (3) Identify your goal, (4) Brainstorm solutions (many, including silly ones), (5) Choose one, (6) Try it, (7) Evaluate. Practice: When your child is stuck, resist solving it for them. Instead, walk through the steps: 'What's the pot? What's the tree? What's bumping?' Let THEM find the back-of-the-tree solution.
sections.Try This: The Anansi Method: Next time you're stuck, write down the problem at the top of a page. Then list FIVE possible solutions — including weird ones. Circle the one you want to try first. Try it. If it doesn't work, circle the next one. You're not failing — you're climbing the tree.
sections.Reflection: When you're stuck on a problem, do you keep trying the same thing harder? Or do you step back and try a different approach? What helps you see the 'back of the tree'?

metadata.source_text: Anansi and the Pot of Wisdom — Ashanti oral tradition, public domain

sections.The Story:
A grandparent sat with their grandchild by the fire.

'There are two wolves inside me,' the grandparent said. 'They fight all the time.'

'One is angry, jealous, full of self-pity, resentful, and afraid. The other is joyful, peaceful, loving, generous, and kind.'

The child's eyes went wide. 'Which one wins?'

The grandparent smiled. 'The one I feed.'

But here's what the simple version misses:

The grandparent DIDN'T say 'kill the angry wolf.' Didn't say 'the angry wolf is bad.' Said: 'They fight all the time.' Both wolves are IN there. Both are real. Both have teeth.

The angry wolf protects you when you're in danger. The kind wolf connects you to others. You need BOTH.

The skill isn't choosing one wolf. It's learning which one to feed in which moment. That's the middle path — not 'always be kind' and not 'always be angry' but 'both are real, and I can choose.'

When someone hurts you: feed the angry wolf enough to protect yourself, then feed the kind wolf so you don't become cruel.

When someone loves you: feed the kind wolf fully, and let the angry wolf rest.

The dialectic: two things that seem opposite can BOTH be true. You can be angry AND loving. You can set a boundary AND be kind. You can say no AND still care.

The wolves don't have to fight. They can learn to run side by side.
sections.The Song:
Two things true at the same time —
Both are real, and both are mine.
Walk the middle, hold them both —
That's the path of wisest growth.
sections.The Skill: This story teaches WALKING THE MIDDLE PATH — the dialectical skill of holding two opposite truths at the same time. Not 'either/or' but 'both/and.' You can be angry and still be kind. You can need help and still be strong. The middle path isn't compromise — it's holding the whole truth.
sections.For Parents: DBT Skill: WALKING THE MIDDLE PATH / DIALECTICS (Interpersonal Effectiveness — Adolescent module). The core dialectic: two seemingly opposite things can both be true. I can love you AND be furious. You can be doing your best AND need to do better. This skill reduces all-or-nothing thinking, which is the driver of most interpersonal conflict. Practice: When your child makes an either/or statement ('She ALWAYS does that!' or 'NOBODY likes me'), gently add 'AND': 'She does that sometimes AND she's also been kind.' Both wolves.
sections.Try This: The Both-Wolves Game: Think of something you feel two ways about. Maybe a friend who's fun AND sometimes mean. Or a rule that's annoying AND keeps you safe. Write both on paper. Can you hold both at the same time without choosing? That's the middle path.
sections.Reflection: Is there something in your life where two opposite things are both true? Can you name both without choosing one?

metadata.source_text: The Two Wolves — oral tradition, public domain (multiple cultural variants)

sections.About: Before you can handle big feelings, you need to SEE them. Mindfulness is the foundation — learning to pay attention to what's actually happening, inside and outside, without rushing to judge or fix. These seven stories teach the What Skills (Observe, Describe, Participate) and the How Skills (Non-Judgmentally, One-Mindfully, Effectively), all rooted in Wise Mind — the place where thinking and feeling meet.
sections.The Song:
Quiet eye, open heart —
Seeing clearly is the start.
sections.For Parents: This module covers DBT Core Mindfulness — the foundation that all other skills build on. Linehan drew these skills from Zen Buddhism and contemplative traditions. The three What Skills (Observe, Describe, Participate) answer 'what do I do to be mindful?' The three How Skills (Non-Judgmentally, One-Mindfully, Effectively) answer 'how do I do it?' Wise Mind integrates everything. If you only work on one module, work on this one.
sections.How to Use: Read one story per week. After each story, practice the 'Try This' for several days before moving to the next. The mantras ('The Song') are designed to be singable and retrievable — practice them until your child can recall them spontaneously.

items.name: The Dark Wood: Distress Tolerance Skills

sections.About: Sometimes the dark comes. You're inside the whale, in the underworld, in the labyrinth. You can't think your way out. You can't fix it. The only skill is SURVIVING without making it worse. These ten stories teach crisis survival: how to stop before you react, accept what is, calm your body, distract wisely, improve the unbearable moment, and show up even when it seems impossible.
sections.The Song:
When the dark wood has no door —
Breathe and bend, you've been here before.
sections.For Parents: This module covers DBT Distress Tolerance — skills for surviving crisis without making things worse. These are NOT problem-solving skills (that's Emotion Regulation). These are 'the building is on fire and I need to survive until the fire trucks arrive' skills. Key insight: the goal is not to feel better, it's to not make things worse. Radical Acceptance is the cornerstone — pain + non-acceptance = suffering.
sections.How to Use: These stories are best read BEFORE crises, not during them. Read them when your child is calm. Practice the body-based skills (TIPP, self-soothe) regularly so they're available during distress. The ice cube exercise from Jonah is an excellent practice.

items.name: The Feeling Compass: Emotion Regulation Skills

sections.About: Emotions are like weather — they come and go, and they're always telling you something. But sometimes the weather report is WRONG. Chicken Little thought the sky was falling. The Wind thought blowing harder would work. These eight stories teach how to understand your emotions, check whether they fit the facts, change them when they don't, accumulate good experiences, witness your feelings without drowning, solve problems creatively, and build a body and life that keeps you less vulnerable to emotional storms.
sections.The Song:
Feelings come like weather too —
I can feel AND choose what to do.
sections.For Parents: This module covers DBT Emotion Regulation — skills for understanding and changing unwanted emotions. Unlike Distress Tolerance (survive the crisis), these skills work on the longer arc: reducing vulnerability, checking interpretations, and acting opposite to unhelpful emotion urges. The ABC PLEASE skills (Build Mastery, Cope Ahead, PLEASE) are preventive. Check the Facts and Opposite Action are in-the-moment skills.
sections.How to Use: Start with Check the Facts (Chicken Little) — it's the most immediately practical. Build Mastery and PLEASE are daily practices. Opposite Action is powerful but requires the child to already have some mindfulness skill (they need to notice the urge before they can reverse it).

items.name: The Talking Circle: Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills

sections.About: Every relationship is a story we tell together. These four stories teach the three goals of interpersonal effectiveness: getting what you need (DEAR MAN), keeping the relationship (GIVE), and keeping your self-respect (FAST). Plus the skill that underlies all relationships: validation — seeing someone as they really are and saying 'yes, that's real.'
sections.The Song:
Say what you need, hear what they say —
Two hearts talking, that's the way.
sections.For Parents: This module covers DBT Interpersonal Effectiveness — skills for navigating relationships. The three sub-skills address different goals: DEAR MAN (get what you want), GIVE (maintain the relationship), FAST (maintain self-respect). The dialectic: all three matter, and sometimes they conflict. Validation is the master skill — research shows that feeling validated reduces emotional arousal faster than almost any other intervention.
sections.How to Use: DEAR MAN (Scheherazade) is great for school-age children learning to advocate for themselves. GIVE (The Giving Tree) opens a conversation about boundaries. FAST (Nasreddin) is crucial for children who over-apologize. Validation (Velveteen Rabbit) is the one to read first — it models what the child needs to RECEIVE before they can offer it to others.

items.name: The Wise Heart: All Skills Together

sections.About: The Wise Heart holds all four chambers together: the Quiet Eye (mindfulness), the Dark Wood (distress tolerance), the Feeling Compass (emotion regulation), and the Talking Circle (interpersonal effectiveness). These aren't four separate skills — they're one skill seen from four directions. You need to SEE clearly before you can SURVIVE. You need to survive before you can UNDERSTAND your feelings. And you need to understand yourself before you can truly CONNECT with others.
sections.The Pattern:
Mindfulness is the ground.
Distress Tolerance is the roots (holding on when the storm comes).
Emotion Regulation is the trunk (growing strong and flexible).
Interpersonal Effectiveness is the branches (reaching toward others).

The whole tree is the Wise Heart.
sections.For Parents: DBT was developed by Marsha Linehan, who drew from Zen Buddhism, behavioral science, and her own lived experience of emotional suffering. The four modules are taught in order for a reason — each builds on the last. But in practice, children will need different skills at different moments. Let them guide you. The mantras ('The Songs') are designed to be singable, repeatable, and retrievable under stress — the Daniel Tiger pattern. Practice them until they become automatic. That's when they work.
sections.The Song:
See it clearly, breathe it through,
Feel it fully, talk it true —
The wise heart knows just what to do.
